items.question: Explain k-Nearest Neighbors in one paragraph.
items.answer: k-NN stores labeled training data and, for a new point x, finds the k training examples closest by a distance metric. For classification it predicts the majority class among those neighbors; for regression it averages their targets. There is no parametric training phase — complexity is controlled mainly by k and preprocessing.

items.question: What is the role of hyperparameter k?
items.answer: k sets how many neighbors vote on each prediction. Small k follows local structure (flexible boundary, higher variance); large k smooths over wider regions (higher bias, lower variance). Tune k on validation data — see Model Complexity for the bias–variance framing.

items.question: Why must features be scaled before k-NN?
items.answer: Distance sums differences across dimensions. A feature with large numeric range dominates Euclidean or Manhattan distance, so neighbors are selected by scale artifacts rather than meaningful similarity. Standardize or normalize first — see Scaling and Normalization.

items.question: What is the time complexity of naive k-NN prediction?
items.answer: O(n·d) per query for n training points and d features — compare x to every stored vector, sort or partial-select k smallest distances, then aggregate labels. Training is O(1) aside from storing data. Indexes or ANN reduce amortized query cost.

items.question: How does k-NN differ from SVM?
items.answer: k-NN is lazy: memorizes all training points and searches neighbors at query time. SVM is eager: learns a decision boundary from support vectors during training with fixed prediction cost. k-NN gives local, example-based explanations; SVM gives a global margin-based boundary.

items.question: How do you handle ties in k-NN classification?
items.answer: Use odd k for binary problems to reduce equal votes, apply distance-weighted voting (closer neighbors count more), or break ties by the neighbor with smallest distance to x. Document the policy in production code.
